section.title: He Heals the Brokenhearted
section.sectionRationale: The second Hallelujah psalm, grouped in three stanzas. It celebrates the LORD who rebuilds Jerusalem and gathers the exiles, heals the brokenhearted, numbers and names every star, sustains creation, and sends forth His word to Jacob, a word later shown to be made flesh, John 1:14.
section.thesis: The same LORD whose power numbers and names every star stoops to heal the shattered heart, provide for all creation, and speak His word to His people, a word that becomes flesh in Jesus Christ.
section.frameNote: Three stanzas, each opening with a summons to praise: the LORD who restores Jerusalem and heals the brokenhearted, verses 1-6; the LORD who sustains creation and delights in those who fear Him, verses 7-11; and the LORD whose word runs swiftly and is declared uniquely to Israel, verses 12-20.

units.label: It is good to sing praises
units.note: the LORD restores Jerusalem and heals the brokenhearted

verses.text: {{haleluyah|Hallelujah}}! How good it is to sing praises to our God, how pleasant and lovely to praise Him!
The LORD builds up Jerusalem; He gathers the exiles of Israel.
He heals the {{nishberei-lev|brokenhearted}} and binds up their wounds.
He determines the number of the stars; He calls them each by name.
Great is our Lord, and mighty in power; His understanding has no limit.
The LORD sustains the humble, but casts the wicked to the ground.

units.label: Sing to the Lord with thanksgiving
units.note: the LORD sustains creation and delights in those who fear Him

verses.text: Sing to the LORD with thanksgiving; make music on the harp to our God,
who covers the sky with clouds, who prepares rain for the earth, who makes grass to grow on the hills.
He provides food for the animals, and for the young ravens when they call.
He does not delight in the strength of the horse; He takes no pleasure in the legs of a man.
The LORD is pleased with those who fear Him, who hope in His {{hesed|loving devotion}}.

units.label: He declares His word to Jacob
units.note: the LORD's provision, command over nature, and His word to Israel

verses.text: Exalt the LORD, O Jerusalem; praise your God, O Zion!
For He strengthens the bars of your gates and blesses the children within you.
He makes peace at your borders; He fills you with the finest wheat.
He sends forth His command to the earth; His {{davar|word}} runs swiftly.
He spreads the snow like wool; He scatters the frost like ashes;
He casts forth His hail like pebbles. Who can withstand His icy blast?
He sends forth His word and melts them; He unleashes His winds, and the waters flow.
He declares His word to Jacob, His statutes and judgments to Israel.
He has done this for no other nation; they do not know His judgments. {{haleluyah|Hallelujah}}!

haleluyah.gloss: An imperative call, praise the LORD. It opens and closes each of the last five psalms of the Psalter, framing the whole collection's final movement as one continuous shout of praise.

nishberei-lev.gloss: Shattered of heart, brokenhearted. The same LORD who numbers and names every star, a display of cosmic power beyond calculation, stoops to heal the shattered heart, an intimate compassion within that same power.

hesed.gloss: Covenant loving devotion, or steadfast love. The LORD delights not in human strength but in those who fear Him and hope in His hesed.

davar.gloss: Word, command, or matter. The LORD's davar governs weather and nations alike, running swiftly to accomplish His will, a word that becomes flesh in John's Gospel.

basic.context: This psalm celebrates the LORD's twin greatness, His cosmic power over the stars and the weather, and His intimate compassion for the brokenhearted and the humble. It moves through three stanzas, each opening with a summons to praise, and closes by noting Israel's unique privilege of receiving God's own word and statutes.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a hymn of praise for God's providential care, historically tied to the LORD rebuilding Jerusalem and gathering the exiles after the Babylonian captivity, but its abiding pattern is trust in the God whose vast power and tender compassion are never in tension.
basic.originalLanguages: The psalm frames its praise with haleluyah, praise the LORD. Verse 3 names the nishberei lev, the brokenhearted, whom God heals. Verse 11 grounds His delight in those who hope in His hesed, covenant love. Verses 15, 18, and 19 speak of His davar, word, running swiftly and being declared uniquely to Jacob.
basic.typology: The LORD who heals the brokenhearted, verse 3, anticipates Jesus, who reads Isaiah 61's promise of healing for the brokenhearted as His own mission, Luke 4:18. The LORD's word that runs swiftly and melts the frost, verses 15-18, anticipates the Word who was with God and was God, and who became flesh, John 1:1-14.
basic.christ: The healer of the brokenhearted, verse 3, and the LORD whose word runs swiftly and accomplishes His will, verses 15-19, are fulfilled in Jesus Christ, the Word made flesh, John 1:14, who heals the brokenhearted, Luke 4:18, and whose word is living and active, Hebrews 4:12.
basic.god: God is shown as both cosmically powerful, numbering and naming every star, and intimately compassionate, healing the shattered heart. He sustains all creation, delights in those who fear Him rather than in human strength, and has uniquely revealed His word and statutes to Israel.
basic.crossReferences: Luke 4:18 shows Jesus claiming Isaiah's promise to heal the brokenhearted, echoing verse 3. John 1:1-14 identifies Jesus as the eternal Word, echoing the psalm's picture of God's word running swiftly. Hebrews 4:12 describes the word of God as living and active, matching this psalm's dynamic, effective davar.
basic.oneStory: The LORD who commands the stars by name and commands the weather by word is the same LORD who binds up the brokenhearted and speaks His word to His people, a word fulfilled and embodied in Jesus Christ, the Word made flesh, who heals what is broken and reigns over all creation.

context.literary: The second of the Psalter's closing Hallelujah psalms, structured in three stanzas of six, five, and nine verses, each opening with a fresh summons to praise and offering reasons drawn from God's care for Jerusalem, for creation, and for Israel in particular.
context.historical: Reflects the post-exilic period, when Jerusalem's walls and gates were being rebuilt, Nehemiah 1-2, and the exiles were being gathered home, giving concrete, recent history to the psalm's opening claim that the LORD builds up Jerusalem and gathers the exiles of Israel.
context.canonical: Continues the Psalter's final movement of praise, pairing the LORD's cosmic power, verse 4, with His tender care, verse 3, a pairing the New Testament resolves fully in the incarnate Word, who is both Creator and healer.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To lead worshippers to praise the LORD both for His unlimited power over creation and for His tender, particular compassion toward the brokenhearted and the humble, and for the unique privilege of receiving His revealed word.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes a specific historical restoration of Jerusalem and gives specific reasons for praise; it prescribes as an abiding pattern praising God for both His vast power and His intimate compassion, and treasuring His revealed word as a unique gift.

debates.question: Does verse 20's claim that God has done this for no other nation exclude the nations from God's care altogether?

views.label: It marks a genuinely unique, exclusive revelation to Israel under the old covenant
views.case: The statutes and judgments in view are the Mosaic covenant, given to Israel alone at Sinai, a historical and covenantal particularity the text plainly states.

views.label: It describes covenant privilege without denying God's broader providential care for all nations, seen earlier in the same psalm, verses 8-9
views.case: The same psalm celebrates God's care for animals and the created order generally; the uniqueness in view is the gift of His written word and covenant, not all divine favor, and this privilege is later extended to all nations in Christ, Ephesians 2:11-13.

debates.bearing: Both readings affirm Israel's unique covenant privilege in this psalm's setting, while the New Testament shows that privilege was never meant to be the final word, since the gospel brings the nations into the same covenant family through Christ.

study.originalLanguages: The psalm's frame, haleluyah, calls all worshippers to praise the LORD. Verse 3 names the nishberei lev, the brokenhearted, healed by God. Verse 11 speaks of hesed, His covenant loving devotion, as the ground of His delight in His people. Verses 15, 18, and 19 use davar, word, for the powerful, effective speech by which God governs weather and reveals Himself to Jacob.

typology.type: The LORD who heals the brokenhearted, verse 3
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in Jesus, who reads Isaiah's promise of healing for the brokenhearted as His own mission, Luke 4:18.

typology.type: The LORD's word that runs swiftly and accomplishes His will, verses 15-19
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in Christ, the Word who was with God and was God, and who became flesh, John 1:1-14, and in the living, active word of God, Hebrews 4:12.

study.christ: Jesus Christ is the fulfillment of this psalm's twin portrait of God. He is the healer of the brokenhearted, claiming Isaiah's promise as His own mission, Luke 4:18, and He is the Word made flesh, John 1:14, the same davar that this psalm shows running swiftly to govern weather and reveal God's statutes to Israel, now embodied and living among us.
study.god: God is shown as both cosmically powerful, He numbers and names every star and commands the weather, and intimately compassionate, He heals the brokenhearted and binds up their wounds. He sustains the humble, delights in those who fear Him rather than in human strength, and has uniquely declared His word and statutes to Israel.

crossReferences.note: Jesus claims Isaiah's promise to heal the brokenhearted, echoing Psalm 147:3.
Identifies Jesus as the eternal Word made flesh, echoing this psalm's picture of God's word running swiftly and accomplishing His will.
Describes the word of God as living and active, matching this psalm's dynamic, effective davar.

study.oneStory: The LORD who commands the stars by name and commands the weather by word is the same LORD who binds up the brokenhearted and speaks His word to His people. That word is fulfilled and embodied in Jesus Christ, the Word made flesh, who heals what is broken in us and reigns over all creation forever.

sermon.title: Singing, Suffering, and Scripture: How God Keeps Us Through Song
sermon.why: Piper works from Psalm 147:1's call to sing praises that are good and fitting, tracing three ways singing relates to suffering, stopped by it, sustaining through it, and following after it forever, showing how this psalm's summons to praise meets believers in real affliction.

quiz.q: According to verses 2-3, what does the LORD do for Jerusalem and the brokenhearted?

options.text: He builds up Jerusalem, gathers the exiles, heals the brokenhearted, and binds up their wounds.
options.note: These verses pair national restoration with personal, intimate healing.

options.text: He rebukes Jerusalem for her sins and scatters her people further.
options.note: The opposite is described; the LORD gathers and restores, He does not scatter here.

options.text: He ignores Jerusalem until a distant future.
options.note: The psalm describes present, active restoration, not future neglect.

options.text: He rebuilds Jerusalem but leaves the people's wounds untouched.
options.note: The text explicitly adds that He also heals the brokenhearted, not only the city.

quiz.q: What does verse 4 say the LORD does with the stars?

options.text: He determines their number and calls each one by name.
options.note: This pictures God's intimate, personal knowledge of the vastness of the cosmos.

options.text: He merely watches them from a distance.
options.note: The verse describes active naming and numbering, not distant observation.

options.text: He leaves their number a mystery even to Himself.
options.note: The verse says the opposite, that He determines their number precisely.

options.text: He created them but no longer takes notice of them.
options.note: The verse emphasizes His ongoing, personal attention, calling each star by name.

quiz.q: In what does the LORD delight, according to verses 10-11?

options.text: Not in the strength of horse or man, but in those who fear Him and hope in His loving devotion.
options.note: The LORD's pleasure rests on reverent trust, not on impressive human or animal strength.

options.text: In powerful armies and swift warhorses.
options.note: Verse 10 explicitly denies that God delights in these things.

options.text: In wealth and impressive human achievement.
options.note: The passage contrasts strength with humble, trusting fear of the LORD, not wealth specifically.

options.text: Primarily in outward religious performance.
options.note: The emphasis falls on inward fear of the LORD and hope in His hesed, not mere outward performance.

quiz.q: What is unique about Israel's relationship to God, according to verses 19-20?

options.text: He has declared His word and statutes to Jacob as He has done for no other nation.
options.note: The psalm closes by naming this covenant revelation as Israel's particular privilege.

options.text: Israel earned this word through perfect obedience.
options.note: The text presents this as a gift of revelation, not a reward for perfect obedience.

options.text: Every nation received the same revelation equally.
options.note: The verse explicitly says the opposite, He has not done this for other nations.

options.text: Israel alone among all peoples will ever know God.
options.note: The verse describes a historical, covenantal uniqueness, which the New Testament shows extending to all nations in Christ.

quiz.q: How does this psalm's picture of God's word connect to the New Testament?

options.text: God's word running swiftly points to the Word made flesh, John 1:14, and the living, active word of God, Hebrews 4:12.
options.note: The psalm's dynamic, effective davar anticipates the incarnate and living Word of the New Testament.

options.text: It only refers to the weather patterns described in the psalm.
options.note: The weather imagery is real, but Scripture develops this word imagery further toward Christ.

options.text: It has no meaningful New Testament connection.
options.note: John's Gospel draws directly on this kind of Old Testament word theology.

options.text: It predicts a specific future political event.
options.note: The connection is theological, God's living word, rather than a specific political prediction.

questions: The LORD heals the brokenhearted and binds up their wounds, verse 3. What wound are you carrying that needs His healing touch today?
He numbers and names every star, yet stoops to the brokenhearted. How does holding those two truths together, God's vastness and His nearness, change how you pray?
The LORD delights not in human strength but in those who fear Him and hope in His loving devotion, verses 10-11. Where are you tempted to trust your own strength rather than His steadfast love?
God declares His word uniquely to His people, verses 19-20. How does having God's word in Scripture shape the way you approach a decision you are facing this week?
This psalm calls for thanksgiving and music, verse 7. What specific thing from this past week deserves a fresh song of thanks to God?

translationNotes.note: Hallelujah renders halelu-yah, opening and closing this psalm and framing the Psalter's final movement of praise.
Brokenhearted renders nishberei lev, literally shattered of heart, stressing inward, emotional devastation rather than only physical affliction.
Pebbles, also rendered crumbs or morsels, describes hail falling like small, hard pieces, an image of God's word made visible in weather.
